metadata.summary: Production self-hosted vector DB: HNSW indexing, payload filtering, quantization, hybrid search, snapshots. Docker on port 6333/6334.

content.title: Qdrant Vector Database
content.summary: Qdrant outperforms Pinecone at production scale for self-hosted workloads (41 QPS @ 50M vectors) and exposes HNSW tuning knobs unavailable in Chroma. Scalar quantization halves RAM without measurable recall loss for most corpora; binary quantization achieves 32x reduction for high-dimensional models. Built-in sparse-vector support eliminates the need for a separate keyword search engine in hybrid pipelines.

why.p: Qdrant outperforms Pinecone at production scale for self-hosted workloads (41 QPS @ 50M vectors) and exposes HNSW tuning knobs unavailable in Chroma. Scalar quantization halves RAM without measurable recall loss for most corpora; binary quantization achieves 32x reduction for high-dimensional models. Built-in sparse-vector support eliminates the need for a separate keyword search engine in hybrid pipelines.

list.item: Standing up a production self-hosted vector store (Docker or Kubernetes)
RAG pipelines needing payload-level filtering alongside vector similarity
Systems requiring hybrid dense+sparse (BM25) search without a separate engine
High-volume indexing (>1M vectors) with memory constraints
Multi-modal retrieval (one point stores text + image vectors as named vectors)
Pipelines requiring point-level snapshots and incremental backups
Prototype/local dev with <50K vectors and no filtering needs — Chroma is simpler and zero-config
Existing PostgreSQL stack where pgvector extension is already present
Teams that need a fully managed SaaS with zero infra ops — use Qdrant Cloud or Pinecone
Requirements for GraphQL or Weaviate-style schema-first data modeling

section.name: installation-and-connection
section.p: Qdrant runs as a Docker container on port 6333 (REST) and 6334 (gRPC). gRPC port 6334 must be explicitly opened in firewall; forgetting it causes silent REST-only mode with 2-3x lower throughput. Install Python client with pip install qdrant-client. Connect via REST (default), gRPC (prefer_grpc=True for 2-3x throughput), or Qdrant Cloud with api_key.

example.title: Docker and client setup
example.code: # docker run -p 6333:6333 -p 6334:6334 \ # -v $(pwd)/qdrant_storage:/qdrant/storage:z qdrant/qdrant # pip install qdrant-client

example.title: Python connection patterns
example.code:
from qdrant_client import QdrantClient from qdrant_client.models import VectorParams, Distance, HnswConfigDiff, OptimizersConfigDiff
# REST (default) client = QdrantClient(host="localhost", port=6333)
# gRPC — 2-3x higher throughput; open port 6334 in firewall client = QdrantClient(host="localhost", port=6334, prefer_grpc=True, timeout=30)
# Qdrant Cloud # client = QdrantClient(url="https://xxx.qdrant.io", api_key="...")
example.note: gRPC port 6334 must be explicitly opened; forgetting it causes silent REST-only mode.

rule.statement: Set on_disk_payload=True for collections storing large text payloads; keeps vectors in RAM while payloads hit disk.
rule.rationale: Text payloads bloat RAM; moving them to disk reduces memory while maintaining vector search speed.

rule.statement: Create payload indexes before bulk ingestion, not after — adding indexes post-ingest locks the collection during the (hours-long) build for large collections.
rule.rationale: Payload index creation is synchronous and can stall searches. Building indexes upfront prevents downtime during production bulk loads.

example.title: Single-vector collection with HNSW
example.code:
client.create_collection( collection_name="documents", vectors_config=VectorParams(size=1536, distance=Distance.COSINE), hnsw_config=HnswConfigDiff(m=16, ef_construct=100), optimizers_config=OptimizersConfigDiff(indexing_threshold=20000), on_disk_payload=True, # large payloads on disk, not RAM )
info = client.get_collection("documents") print(f"Points: {info.points_count}, Indexed: {info.indexed_vectors_count}")

example.title: Named multi-vector collection (multimodal)
Payload indexes for filtered search
example.code:
from qdrant_client.models import PayloadSchemaType
client.create_payload_index("documents", field_name="category", field_schema=PayloadSchemaType.KEYWORD) client.create_payload_index("documents", field_name="created_at", field_schema=PayloadSchemaType.DATETIME) client.create_payload_index("documents", field_name="page", field_schema=PayloadSchemaType.INTEGER) client.create_payload_index("documents", field_name="text", field_schema=PayloadSchemaType.TEXT)

rule.statement: Upsert in batches of 100 points; single-point upserts at scale crush throughput.
rule.rationale: Each upsert call has per-request overhead. Batching amortizes that cost across 100 points.

example.title: Batch upsert
example.code:
from qdrant_client.models import PointStruct
batch_size = 100 points = [] for i, doc in enumerate(documents): points.append(PointStruct( id=i, vector=get_embedding(doc["text"]), payload={"text": doc["text"], "source": doc["source"]}, )) if len(points) >= batch_size: client.upsert(collection_name="documents", points=points) points = [] if points: client.upsert(collection_name="documents", points=points)

example.title: Filtered vector search

rule.statement: Pre-compute BM25 sparse vectors outside Qdrant before upserting; Qdrant stores indices/values but has no built-in tokenizer.
rule.rationale: Qdrant's sparse vector format expects pre-tokenized (indices, values) pairs. Raw text tokenization must happen in the application layer.

example.title: Hybrid dense + sparse with RRF fusion
example.code:
from qdrant_client.models import ( SparseVectorParams, SparseIndexParams, SparseVector, Prefetch, FusionQuery, Fusion )
# Collection with sparse vectors client.create_collection( collection_name="hybrid_docs", vectors_config=VectorParams(size=1536, distance=Distance.COSINE), sparse_vectors_config={"bm25": SparseVectorParams(index=SparseIndexParams(on_disk=False))}, )
# Upsert with both vector types client.upsert( collection_name="hybrid_docs", points=[PointStruct( id=1, vector=dense_embedding, sparse_vectors={"bm25": SparseVector(indices=[100, 500], values=[0.5, 0.3])}, payload={"text": "..."}, )], )
# Hybrid query with Reciprocal Rank Fusion results = client.query_points( collection_name="hybrid_docs", prefetch=[ Prefetch(query=dense_query, using="", limit=20), Prefetch(query=sparse_query, using="bm25", limit=20), ], query=FusionQuery(fusion=Fusion.RRF), limit=10, )

rule.statement: Snapshot restore requires the target collection to be absent first; restoring over an existing collection fails silently.
rule.rationale: Qdrant uses atomic collection swap. The collection to be restored into must not exist to prevent data corruption.

example.title: Creating and restoring snapshots
example.code: snapshot_info = client.create_snapshot(collection_name="documents") snapshots = client.list_snapshots(collection_name="documents") # Restore (collection must not exist) client.recover_snapshot( collection_name="documents", location=f"http://localhost:6333/collections/documents/snapshots/{snapshot_info.name}", )
example.note: Snapshot restore requires the target collection to be absent; restoring over an existing collection fails silently.

rule.statement: Enable rescoring (oversampling=2.0) when using quantization to recover the top-k precision loss.
rule.rationale: Quantization reduces recall. Fetching 2x candidates and rescoring with original float vectors restores near-original precision at the cost of extra memory reads.

rule.statement: Do not use binary quantization for embeddings with fewer than 256 dimensions; recall degrades significantly at low dimensionality.
rule.rationale: Binary quantization compresses each float to a single bit. Low-dimensional vectors have few bits, making the approximation too coarse for reliable similarity.

example.title: Scalar quantization (int8, 4x reduction)
example.code:
from qdrant_client.models import ScalarQuantization, ScalarQuantizationConfig
client.update_collection( collection_name="documents", quantization_config=ScalarQuantization( scalar=ScalarQuantizationConfig( type="int8", quantile=0.99, always_ram=True, ) ), )

example.title: Binary quantization (32x reduction)
example.code:
from qdrant_client.models import BinaryQuantization, BinaryQuantizationConfig
client.update_collection( collection_name="documents", quantization_config=BinaryQuantization( binary=BinaryQuantizationConfig(always_ram=True) ), )

example.title: Search with rescoring
example.code:
from qdrant_client.models import SearchParams, QuantizationSearchParams
results = client.search( collection_name="documents", query_vector=query_embedding, limit=10, search_params=SearchParams( quantization=QuantizationSearchParams( rescore=True, oversampling=2.0, # fetch 20 candidates, rescore to top 10 ) ), )

list.item: Always set on_disk_payload=True for collections storing large text payloads; keeps vectors in RAM while payloads hit disk
Use prefer_grpc=True with timeout=30 in production clients — gRPC throughput is 2-3x REST
Create payload indexes before bulk ingestion, not after — adding indexes post-ingest locks the collection
Set indexing_threshold to 0 during bulk load, then restore to 20000 after — avoids repeated HNSW rebuilds
Use with_vectors=False in search responses unless the caller explicitly needs raw vectors — reduces response size 5-10x
Enable binary quantization with always_ram=True and rescore=True + oversampling=2.0 for balanced speed/recall
Use UUIDs or deterministic integer IDs for points; random IDs cause HNSW locality degradation at scale
Agents must check info.indexed_vectors_count vs info.points_count before querying — unindexed points are searched with brute force and skew latency benchmarks
Sparse vector construction (BM25 token IDs + weights) must happen in the agent's pre-processing step, not inside Qdrant — agents need a tokenizer (e.g., fastembed or custom BM25Encoder)
Score thresholds (score_threshold=0.7) silently return zero results if embeddings are not normalized — agents should validate that embedding magnitudes are ~1.0 for cosine distance
Snapshot restore requires the agent to DELETE the collection first — this is a destructive step that needs human-in-loop confirmation in production
Agent loops that call create_collection on every startup will fail if the collection exists — always guard with get_collection + exception handling
Quantization always_ram=True requires that RAM >= collection vector footprint; agent must query info.vectors_size_bytes and compare against host memory before enabling

template.purpose: QdrantStore wrapper: create collection, batch upsert, filtered search, snapshot
